name: coding
description: Plan, implement, adversarially verify, then fix a code change in the current repository. Plan+verify are read-only; implement+fix have write tools. Memory tools throughout: search prior solutions first, record durable learnings.

stages.prompt: FIRST: search project memory for prior work on this repo — run ctx_search with terms from the task (e.g. the feature area, file names, error text). Prior tickets may have already mapped the conventions, pitfalls, or even solved something similar; use what you find. Then study the repository and produce a concise implementation plan for the runtime task: which files change and how, repo conventions to follow, risks, and how to verify. End with the exact list of files to be created or modified.

Implement the upstream plan for the runtime task. If you hit anything non-obvious (build quirks, hidden coupling, project-specific conventions), check ctx_search for prior fixes before debugging from scratch. Make the code changes now, following the plan and repo conventions. When done, verify with bash: git add -A && git diff --cached --stat, and include that diff stat in your analysis. FINALLY: if you learned something durable about this repo that future tickets must know (a rule, constraint, gotcha, or convention — not task-specific detail), record it with ctx_memory(action="write").

You are reviewing another agent's implementation of the runtime task. The change is unstaged/staged in the working tree — inspect it with: git diff HEAD (or git diff --cached). Adversarially verify it against the original task and the upstream plan: missed requirements, bugs, regressions in touched code paths, convention violations. Run any checks the repo offers via bash (per project memory if ctx_search knows how this repo is verified). Report findings in your control block: verdict pass|fail, with blocking[] (each: file, problem, why it matters) and nits[]. Only genuine defects are blocking — do not invent findings; a sound implementation gets verdict pass with empty blocking.

An adversarial verifier reviewed the implementation; its verdict and findings are in the upstream control data. If verdict is pass with no blocking findings: make NO changes and state in your analysis that verification passed clean. Otherwise: fix every blocking finding now (nits only if trivial), then re-verify with bash: git add -A && git diff --cached --stat, and summarize what you fixed. If a durable repo lesson emerged from a finding, record it with ctx_memory.
